text.title: AI Trust Disclosure Patterns Failure Modes
text.summary: 4 antipatterns observed in real deployments. Each pairs symptom + root cause + concrete fix.

antipattern.symptom: Feature uses 'AI assistant' label without specifying model. Users cannot calibrate trust or differentiate output quality across model swaps.
antipattern.root-cause: Author thought brand consistency required hiding the vendor.
antipattern.fix: Surface vendor + family + version under an info button. Legally required from Feb 2026.

antipattern.symptom: All AI output presented in confident tone regardless of internal confidence score. Users believe outputs that are 60% likely.
antipattern.root-cause: Designer thought hedge phrases would feel 'less premium'.
antipattern.fix: Map confidence bands to badge/tone: >90% confident, 70-90% likely, <70% uncertain (hedge phrase mandatory).

antipattern.symptom: Factual claims have a 'source' link that opens a page unrelated to the claim. Users disengage citations entirely.
antipattern.root-cause: Citation slot filled with the closest-relevance result instead of the actual source.
antipattern.fix: Citation must include passage / quote excerpt; if no source, show explicit 'source unavailable' marker.

antipattern.symptom: AI surface cannot be disabled. Users who distrust AI subscribe-cancel rate spikes after launch.
antipattern.root-cause: Product wanted to enforce AI adoption metric.
antipattern.fix: Offer one-click opt-out that persists across sessions. Track opt-out rate as a north-star metric (lower is healthy, not lower at all costs).
